text:
Admission Date:  1990-12-13       Discharge Date:  1988-06-09


Service:  Oak Circle Center - Mississippi State Hospital MED

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:  The patient is an 82 year old
male nursing home resident with a past medical history of
schizophrenia, mental retardation, questionable coronary
artery disease, questionable atrial fibrillation, who
presented to the Christus Spohn Hospital Corpus Christi South
Emergency Department on 1990-12-13, after a witnessed
aspiration done at his nursing home.  Per his nursing note,
the patient choked on honey thickened food.  Shortly
thereafter, moderate amounts of food were suctioned from his
mouth.  He subsequently became hypoxic with oxygen saturation
of 87% on 100% nonrebreather face mask.  Noted at that time
to also have a heart rate of 154 and a blood pressure of
150/90.  He was transferred to the Nch Healthcare System North Naples Hospital Campus for further evaluation.

In the Emergency Department, the patient was initially
thought to be in congestive heart failure and so was treated
with BiPAP noninvasive ventilation, Lasix 40 mg intravenously
times one, morphine sulfate 2 mg intravenously times one and
Nitroglycerin drip.  Oxygen saturation improved to 96% on
these measures but reportedly his heart rate went up to the
190s.  His tachycardia was thought to be atrial fibrillation
and he was therefore started on a diltiazem drip.  At that
point, his blood pressure then dropped to 60 systolic.

The patient was intubated with plans for cardioversion for
his unstable heart rhythm.  Preintubation arterial blood gas
showed a pH of 7.44, pCO2 45, oxygen saturation 105.  Post
intubation his blood pressure improved to the 120s systolic.
Cardioversion was therefore aborted.  The Medical Intensive
Care Unit team was called to evaluate the patient.  He
continued to have tachycardia with heart rate into the 160s.
He was volume resuscitated with seven liters normal saline to
keep his blood pressure above 100 systolic.

Chest x-ray at that time showed a left lower lobe infiltrate
concerning for aspiration pneumonia.  The patient was started
on ceftriaxone and Flagyl.  He was then transferred to the
Medical Intensive Care Unit.  His Medical Intensive Care Unit
course was notable for a switch of antibiotic coverage to
ceftriaxone and clindamycin.  The patient continued with
episodes of hypotension and the differential diagnosis which
included over diuresis in the Emergency Department versus
hypovolemia versus sepsis.  A central line and arterial line
were placed.

He was aggressively volume resuscitated with intravenous
fluids.  Electrocardiograms were reinterpreted as multifocal
atrial tachycardia and therefore the patient was switched to
oral diltiazem.  Shortly thereafter he self converted to
normal sinus rhythm.  He was weaned off the diltiazem drip on
1980-01-09.  An echocardiogram was performed which showed an
ejection fraction of 50% and it was notable also for left
atrial enlargement.

While intubated, the patient was noted to have increased
thick secretions but he continued to oxygenate and ventilate
well.  He self extubated on 1971-06-25.  Post extubation, he
was noted to be verbally abusive and combative.  Per
collateral information obtained from his nursing home as well
as case manager, this was felt to be the patient's baseline.

On initial presentation, depakote was held because of mental
status changes but in light of his agitation and
combativeness, depakote was reinstituted.  The Psychiatric
consultation service was contactChristopher Myers for medication
recommendations.

The patient was restarted on Depakote, paroxetine, Seroquel,
and trihexyphenidyl.  His Intensive Care Unit course also was
notable for recurrent episode of multiple tachycardia in the
evening of 2009-12-10.  This tachycardia subsequently
resolved into pulmonary edema felt to be secondary to the
patient's diastolic dysfunction.  Therefore, he was diuresed
with Lasix.  A beta blocker was also started.  He continued
to be stable and was transferred out to the regular medicine
floor on 2019-10-17.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1.  Mental retardation.
2.  Paranoid schizophrenia.
3.  History of prostate cancer status post transurethral
resection of the prostate.
4.  History of swallowing disorder with aspiration.
5.  Atrial fibrillation diagnosed in 2015.
6.  Coronary artery disease status post myocardial
infarction, exact year of which is unknown.
7.  History of drug induced parkinsonism.
8.  Status post right hip fracture and replacement.
9.  History of pneumonia.
10. Questionable history of congestive heart failure.

MEDICATIONS PRIOR TO ADMISSION:
1.  Aspirin 81 mg q. day.
2.  Seroquel 100 mg p.o. twice a day.
3.  Depakote 1000 mg q. h.s.
4.  Paxil 10 mg q. day.
5.  Ranitidine 150 mg twice a day.
6.  Trihexyphenidyl 1 mg twice a day.
7.  Colace 100 mg twice a day.

ALLERGIES:   The patient has no known drug allergies but
while in the Intensive Care Unit had a skin reaction to
adhesive tape.

SOCIAL HISTORY:  The patient resides at Adventist Healthcare Behavioral Health & Wellness.  He
moved to Adventist Healthcare Behavioral Health & Wellness from a group home one month prior to
admission.  He is known to be combative at times.  He is
wheelchair bound.  He needs help with most activities of
daily living.  He has a corporate guardian, Christopher Myers Christopher Myers,
who can be reached at 158-376-742615.  He is a
full code.  His alcohol, tobacco and drug history is unknown.
He is oriented to self only at baseline.

FAMILY HISTORY:  Unavailable.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:  Upon admission, vital signs show a
temperature of 97.6 F.; blood pressure 120/70; heart rate 88;
respiratory rate 24; oxygen saturation 84% on room air and up
to 99% on two liters nasal cannula O2.  General appearance:
Well developed, well nourished obese male, comfortable, in no
acute distress.  HEENT:  Normocephalic, atraumatic.  Pupils
equally round and reactive to light.  Sclerae anicteric.
Cheeks flushed.  Mucous membranes were moist.  Edentulous.
Neck is supple, no masses or lymphadenopathy.  Chest wall:
Left subclavian site with some redness and tenderness and
large blister adjacent.  Lungs clear to auscultation
anteriorly and laterally.  Cardiac:  Regular rate and rhythm
with normal S1 and S2, heart sounds, no murmurs, rubs or
gallops.  Abdomen soft, obese, nontender, moderately
distended, positive normoactive bowel sounds.  Extremities
with trace lower extremity edema.  Extremities are warm and
well perfused.  Neurological:  Moving all extremities,
oriented to self only at baseline.

LABORATORY:  Pertinent labs and radiological studies:  Labs
on transfer to the floor from the Intensive Care Unit showed
complete blood cell count with white blood cell count of 8.2,
hematocrit 26.3, platelets 265.  Serum chemistries showed
sodium of 139, potassium 3.6, chloride 104, bicarbonate 30,
BUN 18, creatinine 0.6, glucose of 91, calcium 7.9,
phosphorus 3.1, magnesium 2.0.  Arterial blood gas on the day
of transfer showed a pH of 7.40, carbon dioxide 47, oxygen
86.  Valproate level was 33.

EKG showed atrial fibrillation versus multi-focal atrial
tachycardia at 144 beats per minute.  Normal axis and
intervals.  A Q wave was noted in lead III.  There were no
acute ST-T wave changes compared with previous studies.

Lower extremity ultrasound on 2010-09-07, demonstrated no
evidence of deep venous thrombosis.  Chest x-ray also from
2010-09-07, demonstrated a continued cardiac enlargement with
peri-hilar haziness and vascular engorgement consistent with
congestive heart failure. Also noted was a persistent left
lower lobe opacity felt to be his aspiration pneumonia.

BRIEF SUMMARY OF HOSPITAL COURSE:
1.  ASPIRATION PNEUMONIA:  The patient will be continued on
clindamycin and ceftriaxone for a total of a 14 day course
started on 1990-12-13, while in the Intensive Care Unit.  He
was maintained on aspiration precautions.  He underwent a
bedside swallowing evaluation and recommended keeping the
patient on thin liquids and further evaluation with a video
speech and swallow study.

The video speech and swallow demonstrated the patient to have
a moderate oropharyngeal dysphagia with poor control of
liquids allowing them to spill prematurely into the pharynx
resulting in aspiration of thin liquids.  He also had
oropharyngeal weakness with resultant residue resulting in
aspiration of nectar thick liquids as well.  Therefore per
the Speech and Swallow recommendations the patient's diet was
modified to puree solids, nectar thick liquids with
aspiration precautions and one to one assistance and
supervision for meals allowing for the fact that he will most
likely continue to aspirate.

He was continued on oxygen to keep his oxygen saturations
greater than 88% and had supportive care with metered dose
inhalers and pulmonary toilet as needed.

2.  HYPOTENSION:  On transfer from the Intensive Care Unit to
the Floor, the patient had episodes of hypotension which was
felt to be secondary to over diuresis versus sepsis
initially.  This responded well to volume resuscitation.
There was some concern that perhaps his hypotension was
medication related.  In light of this, initially his beta
blocker was held while his blood pressure was labile.  After
volume expansion with fluids and blood products, the
patient's blood pressure stabilized and beta blocker was
reinstituted.

3.  MULTIFOCAL ATRIAL TACHYCARDIA:  The patient had
intermittent episodes of multifocal atrial tachycardia on
admission as well as while in the Intensive Care Unit.  He
self converted to normal sinus rhythm.  He was started on a
low dose beta blocker for rate control.

4.  CONGESTIVE HEART FAILURE WITH DIASTOLIC DYSFUNCTION:  The
patient underwent an echocardiogram which demonstrated
elongation of the left atrium and mild global left
hypokinesis.  The left ventricular ejection fraction was 50%.
There was trivial mitral and tricuspid regurgitation.  The
patient had episodes of pulmonary edema status post these
episodes of multifocal atrial tachycardia likely being
diastolic dysfunction.

In light of his congestive heart failure, he was started on a
low dose beta blocker and was diuresed as needed with Lasix.

5.  ANEMIA:  Status post volume resuscitation with over seven
to eight liters of normal saline.  The patient demonstrated a
falling hematocrit value.  This was felt to be secondary to
dilution from volume resuscitation.  He was transfused one
unit of packed red blood cells which brought his hematocrit
up to a stable level greater than 28.

6.  CONSTIPATION:  The patient had to be manually disimpacted
times two while in the Medical Intensive Care Unit.  In light
of this, he should be continued on an aggressive bowel
regimen as an outpatient.

7.  SCHIZOPHRENIA:  The patient was seen by the Psychiatric
consultation service after extubation.  They recommended
continuing him on Seroquel, Paxil, trihexyphenidyl and
Depakote; however, it was noted that this regimen often led
to over sedation in the patient.  This was of concern
especially in light of his aspiration risks.  It is
recommended that if at all possible that these medications
should be titrated down to baseline levels or to allow for
increased alertness and participation in feeding and care.

8.  FLUIDS, ELECTROLYTES AND NUTRITION:  The patient should
be maintained on a pureed solid nectar thick diet with
aspiration precautions.

9.  CODE STATUS:  At this time, the patient is still full
code per his corporate guardian, however, they are
reassessing the situation and will discuss with his future
health care providers as needed.

CONDITION AT DISCHARGE:   Fair, afebrile.  Hemodynamically
stable.  He continues to aspiration on swallowing studies.

DISCHARGE STATUS:  The patient was discharged to an extended
care facility.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSES:
1.  Aspiration pneumonia.
2.  Hypoxia due to aspiration pneumonia.
3.  Respiratory failure requiring intubation.
4.  Multi-focal atrial tachycardia.
5.  Paranoid schizophrenia.
6.  Prostate cancer status post transurethral resection of
the prostate.
7.  Swallowing disorder with history of aspiration.
8.  History of atrial fibrillation.
9.  Coronary artery disease status post myocardial
infarction.
10. Drug induced Parkinsonism.
11. Status post right hip fracture and replacement.
12. History of pneumonia.
13. Congestive heart failure with diastolic dysfunction.

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS:
1.  Colace 100 mg p.o. twice a day.
2.  Senna 8.6 mg one p.o. twice a day.
3.  Lactulose 30 ml p.o. three times a day as needed for
constipation.
4.  Valproate 250 mg p.o. four times a day of the elixir.
5.  Seroquel 100 mg p.o. twice a day.
6.  Dulcolax 10 mg p.o. twice a day as needed for
constipation.
7.  Combivent one to two puffs inhaled q. two hours as needed
for shortness of breath and wheezing.
8.  Paroxetine 10 mg p.o. q. day.
9.  Trihexyphenidyl 1 mg p.o. twice a day.
10. Tylenol 325 mg, one to two tablets p.o. q. four to six
hours as needed.
11. Lopressor 12.5 mg p.o. twice a day.
12. Cefpodoxime 200 mg p.o. q. 12 hours for four days for a
total of 14 day course.
13. Clindamycin 600 mg p.o. q. eight hours for four days for
completion of a 14 day course.
14. Haldol 1 mg p.o. twice a day as needed for agitation.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
1.  The patient should follow-up with his primary care
physician, Rogers. Frank Mcgee and schedule an appointment
at 652-272-2133 as needed for any concerns or recurrent
symptoms.




                            Kathryn Woods, M.D.  48237

Dictated By:Mcclure
